hq_location: Toronto, ON (HQ) + Atlanta, GA (US ops); engineering in Pakistan
specialty: AR (mechanical) primary; shop management SaaS with bundled marketing add-on
typical_monthly_usd: $179-$350/mo core SMS + $149/mo marketing module add-on = ~$300-$500/mo typical single-location bundle [uncertain]
setup_fee_usd: One-time onboarding fee charged on top of annual contract; exact amount not published; refundable only if customer completes group training classes [uncertain]
min_ad_spend_usd: N/A — no paid ad management included; SMS/email/review focused only
contract_length_months: 12 (annual) standard; monthly available but rare; auto-renewal
cancellation_policy: 60-day written notice required BEFORE annual renewal date; miss the window and customer is locked in another full year. Multiple Capterra/Diagnostic Network/Trustpilot reviews report sales reps minimize this term during demo.
asset_ownership: Website built on AutoLeap platform — customer does NOT own the site or code post-cancellation. Domain typically registered by customer (kept). Customer data exportable but no native portability of templates/content. SMS subscriber list ownership unclear. [uncertain]
pricing_transparency: Quote-only — starting price $179/mo published, all tiers and add-ons gated behind sales demo. Marketing module $149/mo referenced in promo copy.
known_pain_points: 12-month auto-renew with 60-day cancellation window — most-cited complaint across Capterra, Diagnostic Network, Trustpilot
Setup fee charged on top of annual contract; only refundable via group training classes
Features demoed but not available at launch ('80% baked' — especially integrated parts ordering)
Over 50% of marketing SMS messages reportedly fail delivery; vendor blames customer spam blockers
Sales team uses high-pressure tactics to lock in annual deal
Pricing not transparent; many add-on costs surface post-signature
Marketing module is SMS/email/review only — no paid media management
Website is platform-locked (customer cannot take it on cancellation)
notable_clients: Bain Capital Ventures-backed; 4.8/5 across 743+ Capterra reviews; thousands of independent AR shops
pros: Integrated stack — shop management + marketing + reviews + website in one tool
Strong UI/UX (consistent praise in Capterra reviews)
Built-in SMS reminders and Google review request automation
Well-funded ($48M raised, Bain Capital + Bessemer + Advance Venture Partners) — vendor stability
Auto repair vertical specialist (built FOR shops, not generic SaaS)
cons: Annual lock-in + 60-day notice = highest contract friction in segment (worse than Hibu's 15-day)
Setup fee + annual prepay creates high switching cost
No paid media management (no Google Ads, no Meta Ads) — would need separate vendor
Marketing module is SMS/email/review tools, NOT acquisition marketing
Website is platform-locked — same lock-in problem as Hibu and RSW
Collision (CR) workflows weaker than mechanical (AR) — built for general repair shops
koffman_relevance: Direct competitor to Tekmetric and Shopgenie at the SMS core, which means it would conflict with Koffman's existing Turnkey + Shopgenie stack rather than replacing Hibu. AutoLeap is shop management + retention marketing, NOT acquisition marketing — does not replace Hibu's Google Ads / website / display layer. If Koffman swapped Shopgenie for AutoLeap they would gain a cleaner SMS/email/review experience but lose Turnkey's call coaching and CR workflow, plus take on a 12-month auto-renew with 60-day notice (worse contract terms than Turnkey's likely 30-day). PSG would NOT recommend AutoLeap as a Hibu replacement — they solve different problems. Could be considered as a Tekmetric/Shopgenie alternative ONLY if Koffman wants tighter native marketing inside their SMS, but contract friction is a deal-breaker vs Tekmetric's month-to-month posture.
uncertain: base_monthly_high_usd exact ceiling for top tier
typical_monthly_usd for single-location with marketing add-on
asset_ownership of SMS subscriber list and website content
whether Koffman currently has any AutoLeap exposure
